coredata.dc:title: Integration of nitrogen-doped carbon dots onto active layer of forward osmosis membrane for highly efficient antibacteria and enhanced membrane performances
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Wen-Sheng
Wu, Pingping
Xiao, Bihua
Chen, Xia
Kong, Weili
Li, Yaru
Liu, Zhaoming
Wang, Yaqin
coredata.dc:description: Biofouling severely hampered the development of thin-film composite (TFC) forward osmosis (FO) membrane. Herein, novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-C-dots) with fluorescence and long-lived afterglow dual-emission were fabricated using Na2EDTA and urea as co-precursors. The N-C-dots aqueous solution can synchronously produce superoxide anion radical (O2 -ˑ) and singlet oxygen (1O2) under light irradiation, exhibiting immense nanoenzyme activities. The membranes (PEI-M and N-C-dots/PEI-M) were prepared by grafting PEI and N-C-dots/polyethyleneimine nanocomposites (N-C-dots/PEI) onto the active layer of pristine TFC FO membrane (Control-M), respectively. The N-C-dots/PEI-M exhibited much better hydrophilicity and smoothness than Control-M and PEI-M, resulting in a higher water permeability. Most significantly, N-C-dots/PEI-M possessed a unique dual-mode biofouling resistance, which responded to the potentials of N-C-dots as oxidase mimic and photosensitizer. In dynamic sewage treatment test, N-C-dots/PEI-M displayed much lower reduction in water flux and much better anti-biofouling behavior. Results above demonstrated that the modification strategy with N-C-dots/PEI is highly effective, pioneering a new horizon for the highly efficient inhibition of biofilm growth and biofouling development under visible light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (C-dots)
Oxidase mimics
Photosensitizer
Biofouling resistance
TFC FO membrane

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Antibacterial property and mechanism of N-C-dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Anhui Education Department ( 2022AH050239 ) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project ( 2017M612091 ). The authors also thank PhD research startup foundation of Anhui Jianzhu University (2020QDZ23) and National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 52003003 ) for ﬁnancial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern (A) and FTIR spectra (B) of N-C-dots. XPS spectrum of N-C-dots (C). The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (D) O1s (E) and N1s (F).

Growth of bacteria treated with N-C-dots in darkness (A) and light irradiation (B). The TEMP (C) and DMPO (D) spin-trapping ESR spectra of N-C-dots after the deduction of baseline. The schematic illustration of dual-mode antibacteria (E) and photosensitization Jablonski energy level diagram of N-C-dots (F).
The fluorescence (A) and ATR-FTIR (B) spectra of Control-M, PEI-M and N-C-dots/PEI-M. Water contact angles (C) and AFM images (D) of the Control-M, PEI-M, and N-C-dots/PEI-M.
FO performances of the Control-M, PEI-M and N-C-dots/PEI-M. (2 M NaCl aqueous solution and DI water were used as the draw solution and feed solution, respectively).
The images of bacteria grown on the agar plate of the Control-M, PEI-M and N-C-dots/PEI-M for colony counting in darkness and light irradiation, respectively.

Variation of the water flux of the Control-M, PEI-M, and N-C-dots/PEI-M with operation time during the dynamic test with E. coli as a model bacteria (A). Dynamic FO water flux of the Control-M, PEI-M and N-C-dots/PEI-M before the dynamic antibacteria test and after cleaning (B).

Dynamic FO simulated sewage tests of the Control-M, PEI-M and N-C-dots/PEI-M. (The NaCl solution and SA solution were used as a draw solution and a feed one, respectively; the Jw/Jw,0 ratios were taken with 40 min interval during the test) (A). Dynamic FO water flux of the Control-M, PEI-M and N-C-dots/PEI-M before fouling and after cleaning (B).

The schematic illustration of the synthesis of N-C-dots (A), conjugations of N-C-dots and N-C-dots/PEI onto PEI (B), and the active layer of TFC membrane (C), respectively, and antibacterial tests under light irradiation (D).
Intrinsic transport properties of the Control-M, PEI-M, and N-C-dots/PEI-M.

head.ce:title: Integration of nitrogen-doped carbon dots onto active layer of forward osmosis membrane for highly efficient antibacteria and enhanced membrane performances

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Anhui Jianzhu University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biofouling severely hampered the development of thin-film composite (TFC) forward osmosis (FO) membrane. Herein, novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-C-dots) with fluorescence and long-lived afterglow dual-emission were fabricated using Na2EDTA and urea as co-precursors. The N-C-dots aqueous solution can synchronously produce superoxide anion radical (O2 -ˑ) and singlet oxygen (1O2) under light irradiation, exhibiting immense nanoenzyme activities. The membranes (PEI-M and N-C-dots/PEI-M) were prepared by grafting PEI and N-C-dots/polyethyleneimine nanocomposites (N-C-dots/PEI) onto the active layer of pristine TFC FO membrane (Control-M), respectively. The N-C-dots/PEI-M exhibited much better hydrophilicity and smoothness than Control-M and PEI-M, resulting in a higher water permeability. Most significantly, N-C-dots/PEI-M possessed a unique dual-mode biofouling resistance, which responded to the potentials of N-C-dots as oxidase mimic and photosensitizer. In dynamic sewage treatment test, N-C-dots/PEI-M displayed much lower reduction in water flux and much better anti-biofouling behavior. Results above demonstrated that the modification strategy with N-C-dots/PEI is highly effective, pioneering a new horizon for the highly efficient inhibition of biofilm growth and biofouling development under visible light.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The separation membrane based on the forward osmosis (FO) process, named FO membranes, is one of the promising separation technologies for wastewater treatment. FO membrane has the advantages of featuring energy efficiency, low cost, and high sustainability [1–9]. Till now, many efforts have been made to develop FO membranes for practical applications. Among them, thin film composite polyamide (TFC-PA) FO membranes with excellent chemical inertness and the favorable salt rejection have become one of the research hot-spots [10,11]. However, membrane fouling, especially biofouling and internal concentration polarity (ICP), can severely lead to the declined water flux and service life of FO membranes. Worst of all, biofouling caused by the deposition and growth of living organisms on the surface or in the pores of FO membranes makes the irreversible decrease of membrane separation performance [12,13]. Therefore, membrane fouling is one of the most serious challenges for the application of FO membranes.

To solve this problem, the strategies of chemical cleaning and inhibiting bacterial proliferation have been widely attempted to prevent the development of biofoulings on FO membranes [14–17]. Regarding inhibiting bacterial proliferation, some fillers such as Ag nanoparticles [18–21], Ag/Cr(III)-based MOFs [22–26], phytic acid-metal complexes [27,28], etc., have been reported to be coupled onto the surface or into the substrate film of FO membrane for mitigating the biofouling via the incessant release of metal ions. Besides, it was reported that nano-sized graphene oxide (GO) can generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) for biofouling-resistance under light irradiation. For instance, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)-GO hydrogels were modified onto the surface of TFC-FO membrane, the prepared membrane displayed the significantly enhanced antibacterial properties, opening up a promising method for efficiently suppressing biofilm and biofouling [29]. However, the nanoparticles such as those mentioned above are normally expensive and uneconomic to be used in a large scale. In addition, the introduced nanoparticles in the membrane are liable to run off during membrane separation process, resulting in the loss of preparation efficacy and even secondary pollution. Therefore, regarding improving the antifouling properties of FO membranes, the development of antibacterial activity nanomaterials which are low-cost and stable for the surface modification of FO membranes is of great urgency.

Nanozymes are nanomaterials with enzyme-like activities, which can address the inherent shortcomings of conventional enzymes such as fragility, high cost, and difficulty in mass production [30,31]. The enzyme-like activity originates from the unique physicochemical properties of nanomaterials, providing great possibilities for rational design and future applications. In recent years, nanozymes have attracted tremendous interest, and many nanomaterials have been found to be mimics of enzyme. Among them, carbon dots (C-dots) have been expected to be the next-generation artificial enzymes to mimic the highly evolved catalytic center of natural enzymes [32,33]. A wide range of enzyme-like activities of C-dots such as peroxidase [34–37], oxidase [38], superoxide dismutase (SOD) [39], photosensitive activity [40,41], etc., have been reported. Furthermore, C-dots have been proved to be the co-catalytic active sites and the excellent electron acceptor/donor units, which showed a unique ability to improve the catalytic efficiency for H2O2 green fabrication [42] and DNA cleavage [43]. Metal ions mediating or doping strategies can significantly increase the efficiency of electron transfer to improve the properties of enzyme mimics [44–46]. Enzyme-like activities endowed C-dots with more unique properties to enhance the prospect of their biological applications. However, the application of C-dots as enzyme mimics, especially in multiple enzyme mimics, for highly efficient antibacteria to hamper biofilm growth and biofouling development is still rarely investigated. Fortunately, a few C-dots displayed multiple enzyme-like activities including photosensitization. The good photosensitized oxygen activation makes the C-dots a promising oxidase/peroxidase-mimicking nanozyme for photodynamic therapy and other applications [47,48].

To improve the anti-fouling properties of the FO membranes, novel C-dots containing amino groups were prepared in this study. Briefly, Na2EDTA and urea were utilized as carbon resources to synthesize N-doped C-dots (N-C-dots) under the inert atmosphere through a thermal polymerization method. The photo- physicochemical properties and the concrete species of reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated under light irradiation of N-C-dots were investigated in detail. Also, the generation mechanisms of ROS were considered. To reveal the enormous potential of N-C-dots as enzyme mimics for biofouling resistance, the N-C-dots were firstly reacted with PEI to fabricate N-C-dots/PEI nanocomposites, then grafted onto a TFC FO membrane to prepare an antibacterial membrane (N-C-dots/PEI-M). For the sake of contrast, the membrane (PEI-M) by grafting PEI onto the pristine membrane (Control-M) was also prepared. Their performances of the as-prepared membrane in FO mode such as surface hydrophilicity, contact angle, structure parameter, water flux, salt rejection, etc., were evaluated. Additionally, the dynamic sewage treatment test was conducted to demonstrate the anti-fouling potential of N-C-dots/PEI-M.

In brief, 0.16 g of urea and 0.285 g of Na2EDTA were mixed in an agate mortar, and then fully grinded for 30 min. The resultant mixture was transferred to a crucible prior to heat to 350 °C for 60 min by 5 °C min−1 of heating rate in an inert atmosphere. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the brown black product was dispersed in distilled water and supersonic vibration for 30 min. Subsequently, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to remove the large particles. The supernatant was concentrated and then freeze-dried for further use.

Control-M was prepared according to our previous report [49]. A hybrid technique was employed to conjugate N-C-dots onto PEI. EDC/NHS (4 mM) was dissolved in 10 mg mL−1 of N-C-dots aqueous solution by sonication for 20 min at pH 6.0. PEI was then added to form a mixed solution-containing 40 μg mL−1 of PEI. The resulting solution reacted for 30 min to obtain N-C-dots/PEI nanocomposites. Similarly, coupling PEI and N-C-dots/PEI onto the active layer of TFC FO membranes was conducted. The membrane was sandwiched between a backing plate and a frame, and the active layer was antitropic to the backing plate. Subsequently, a certain amount of PEI or N-C-dots/PEI solution was immediately added to the aqueous solution for 30 min. The obtained PEI-M and N-C-dots/PEI-M were stored in ultrapure (UP) water at 10 °C for further use after the removals of unreacted PEI and N-C-dots/PEI by rinsing with UP water for three times.

The FO performance was evaluated using a cross-flow system mainly consisting of a white LED lamp (40 mW cm−1, λ ≥ 360 nm) as the visible light source and a transparent acrylic cell with 9.62 cm2 effective membrane surface area. The distance between the illuminant and membrane is 30 cm. Membranes were tested in AL-FS orientation with 1.0 M NaCl and UP water used as draw solution and feed one, respectively. The flow rate of both feed and draw solutions was maintained at 500 mL/min during the FO membrane performance tests. The membrane water flux (Jw, L m−2 h−1) was measured by the volume change of permeation, which was automatically recorded at a constant time interval by a digital weight balance (Eq. 1). The reverse solute flux (Js, g m−2 h−1) across the membrane was calculated by monitoring the feed solution salinity using a conductivity meter (Eq. 2). Eqs. 1 and 2 are as follows:
(1)
J
w
=
ΔV
Effecttive membrane surface area × Δt
(2)
J
s
=
Δ (
C
t
V
t
)
Effecttive membrane surface area × Δt
where ΔV, C
t, and V
t are the volume change of permeation over the test time Δt, salt concentration, and volume of the feed solution, respectively.
More details about the physical characterization of materials and biofilm inhibition tests were described in the support information materials.

In this work, the N-C-dots were synthesized using Na2EDTA and urea as carbon resources by a thermal polymerization method as illustrated in Scheme 1A. The morphology, surface charge, and crystalline state of the obtained N-C-dots were characterized to study the physicochemical properties. The details on apparatus and characterization are provided (3. Apparatus and characterization in Supporting Information (SI)). The zeta-potential is measured at approximate 2.4 eV (Fig. S1 in SI), revealing that the N-C-dots are positively charged. This result manifests less -COOH groups than -NH2 ones on the surface of N-C-dots. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image shows that N-C-dots are typical sphere-like morphology, and their average diameters are all around 3–5 nm. The corresponding particle size distribution diagram well coincides with the TEM result (Fig. S2 in SI). The crystalline structure of N-C-dots was further analyzed by XRD. As shown in Fig. 1A, a broad diffraction peak at 26.7° and a shoulder one at 40.6° were observed in the XRD pattern, corresponding to (002) and (100) crystal planes of carbon [50], respectively. The result reveals that the N-C-dots are amorphous structures, attributing to the decoration of –COOH and –NH2 groups on the surface of the C-dots [51].

The FTIR spectra were recorded to analyze the functional groups on the surface of the N-C-dots. Also, the formation of N-C-dots was simply elucidated by comparing the difference between the FTIR curves of N-C-dots and mixture of Na2EDTA and urea. As shown in Fig. 1B, the breathing bands at 807 and 894 cm−1 exhibit the existence of s-triazine structure, which is the characteristic absorption of the triazine ring. The corresponding C–NH–C skeletal vibration at 1038 cm−1 was found [52].

The stretching vibration at 807 cm−1 is intensely observed due to the larger curvature in nanoparticles [53]. The alternate C-N and CN absorptions at 1304 and 1577 cm−1 in the triazine ring, respectively, were observed [52]. The absorption at 1597 and 1645 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of CC, CN and CO, while the unlabeled bands at 1238 and 1410 cm−1 correspond to the aromatic C-N stretching vibrations [54]. The broadband at 3190 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching modes of –OH/-NHx groups [55]. Significantly, a large amount of absorption at 900–1700 cm−1 and 3300–3450 cm−1 involved in the abundant –COOH group and -OH and –NH2 ones in the curve of the mixture of Na2EDTA and urea, respectively, disappeared or weakened in that of N-C-dots, indicating the occurrence of thermal polymerization reaction for forming N-C-dots.

The elemental composition, chemical structure, and functional groups of the N-C-dots were characterized by XPS. XPS survey scan curve of N-C-dots exhibits three characteristic peaks at 285.4 eV, 401.5 eV, and 533.7 eV, responding to the three elements of C, N, and O, respectively. The corresponding composition ratios are 49.5%, 38.43%, and 12.07%, respectively (Fig. 1C). The high-resolution C1s spectrum contains three well-defined peaks at 284.7, 285.6, and 288.5 eV, which is corresponding to C–C/CC, C–O and N–CN bonds, respectively (Fig. 1D) [55]. The high-resolution XPS O1s curve can be divided into two peaks, which are assigned to C-OH (532.4 eV) and CO (533.0 eV), respectively (Fig. 1E). The high-resolution N1s spectrum includes three peaks at 399.4, 400.5, and 401.8 eV, which are related to CN-C, N-(C)3, and C-N, respectively (Fig. 1F) [54,55]. The results from FTIR and XPS reveal that triazine overring was successfully doped into the C-dots, and the functional groups such as –COOH and –NH2 anchored on the surface of N-C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial property and mechanism of N-C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The N-C-dots can emit brightly blue fluorescence with ∼2.62 ns of average lifetime and ∼14.3% of quantum yield (QY). Meanwhile, the afterglow with ∼9.2 ms of average lifetime of the powder of N-C-dots was also observed (Optical properties and RTP origin of N-C-dots in SI). In consideration of the long-lived afterglow emission of our N-C-dots, and the incompact structure of C-dots prepared by the bottom-up approach, the energy transferred from the excited triplet state (T1) to dissolved oxygen to sensitize oxygen (1O2) for photodynamic antibacterial chemotherapy is possible. Therefore, the study on the potential of N-C-dots as a photosensitizer for antibacterial application was carried out. E. coli was cocultured with 8.0 mg L−1 of N-C-dots for 1.0 h under light, then plated for the visualization. Meanwhile, for comparison, a similar protocol without N-C-dots only was adopted. The results show that the number of bacterial colonies without N-C-dots ( Fig. 2A) was far more than that with N-C-dots (Fig. 2B). The antibacterial efficiency of N-C-dots was over 95% via quantitative analysis, which indicated that the N-C-dots owned an enormous potential as a nanozyme. Meanwhile, the inhibition zone test was also carried out. The inhibition strength for the inhibitory materials was represented by the diameter of the inhibition zone. Specifically, the filter paper was selected with uniform texture. Then, it was cut into discs with the same diameter, dried after sterilization, soaked in the sample to be tested. E. coli were distributed over an agar plate with a sterilized coating stick. Then sample treated filter paper were placed on the agar plate to incubate at 37 ℃ in darkness and light conditions for 24 h, respectively, before measuring the size of the inhibition zone. The results show that, under the light, blank control had no antibacterial effect at all, antibiotic had an antibacterial ring of 33.8–34.3 mm and C-dots had an antibacterial zone of 20.9–21.6 mm in the light (Fig. S9 in SI). This confirms that the N-C-dots prepared in this experiment have apparently antibacterial properties in the light. This results above indicate that the N-C-dots had excellent antibacterial properties.

On the basis of low oxygen photosensitization efficiency, the highly efficient antifouling performances displayed by N-C-dots exceeded our expectations. It is of great necessity to explore the concrete biofouling resistance of N-C-dots. The chromogenic substrate of TMB was employed as a substrate to investigate the enzyme-like activity of N-C-dots by recording the absorbance change at 652 nm of the oxidized TMB. The result shows that N-C-dots can readily oxidize TMB under light irradiation (Fig. S10 in SI), indicating the generation of ROS such as 1O2, O2 -ˑ, OHˑ, etc., by the catalysis of N-C-dots. The TEMP and DMPO spin-trapping electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra of N-C-dots were performed to confirm which species were involved in the TMB oxidation. As well as the typical quadruple signal (1:1:1:1) of 1O2 (Fig. 2C), the typical triplet signal (1:1:1) assigning to O2 -ˑ was merely presented in the ESR spectrum, confirming that the generation of O2 -ˑ, that is our N-C-dots can be used as oxidase but peroxidase mimics (Fig. 2D). Did the hole take part in the TMB oxidation? When isopropanol was used as a hole scavenger, the almost unchanged TMB oxidation degree demonstrated that the hole was not the intermediate in the catalytic reaction (Datum not shown). Therefore, it can be said that the excellent antibacterial effect of N-C-dots shown in Fig. 2B originated from the oxidase-like activity and oxygen sensitization function of N-C-dots under light irradiation (Fig. 2E). Herein, Jablonski energy level diagram can be performed to describe this unique dual-mode antibacterial essence: the electrons in the valence band (S0) were excited to the conduction band (S1) by light, thereafter, partial electrons got back to S0, the blue fluorescence of N-C-dots was observed. Partial electrons were captured by oxygen to generate O2 -ˑ, resulting in the oxidase-like activity of N-C-dots. The rest of the electrons transmitted to the triplet state (T1) through inetrsystem crossing (ISC), if they returned to S0 by irradiation relaxation, the long-lived afterglow was observed; if the dissolved oxygen obtained the energy of triplet excitons by energy transfer pathway, leading to the birth of 1O2 (Fig. 2F).

Biofouling of the pressure driven membrane has become a widely concerned issue. So far, many protocols have been conducted to weaken or eliminate the adherency of microorganisms onto the membrane surface to lower maintenance costs and service life of membranes. On the basis of the great antibacterial potential of N-C-dots described above, conjugating N-C-dots onto TFC FO membrane will be a decent protocol for biofouling resistance. Therefore, N-C-dots were firstly coupled to PEI catalyzed by EDC/NHS to form N-C-dots/PEI nanocomposites (Scheme 1B). Hereafter, the residual amine groups of N-C-dots/PEI were bonded with the activated carboxyl groups of the active layer to obtain N-C-dots/PEI-M (Scheme 1C). For the sake of comparison, the Control-M and PEI-M were also fabricated.

Subsequently, to verify the successful conjugation of N-C-dots onto the surface of FO membrane, the fluorescence spectra, ATR-FTIR, contact angle, and AFM were employed to characterize the Control-M, PEI-M, and N-C-dots/PEI-M. The Control-M and PEI-M can emit fluorescence at 467 nm, which is rooted in the aromatic groups of the active layer. The fluorescence intensity of PEI-M is far stronger than that of Control-M, which can be attributed to the fact that the abundant amine groups of PEI as a hydrogen donor can immobile the emissive center to efficiently inhibit the nonradiative relaxation. The fluorescence at 430 nm and a shoulder peak at 500 nm of N-C-dots/PEI-M were observed. The former is the emission of N-C-dots, the latter is in good agreement with that of Control-M and PEI-M, indicating that both PEI and N-C-dots/PEI were successfully conjugated onto the surface of the Control-M ( Fig. 3A). The ATR-FTIR results show that the peaks at 1540, 1607, and 1650 cm−1 are the C-N stretching, N − H bending and CO stretching of amide, respectively (Fig. 3B) [57]. The enhanced signals of the three groups above in ATR-FTIR curve of N-C-dots/PEI-M evidenced that the successful coupling of nanocomposites onto the membrane surface. In particular, the characteristic absorption at 905 cm−1 of triazine gave the same result.

As depicted in Fig. 3C, the Control-M possessed the least hydrophilic surface with the contact angle of 77.9°. The surface hydrophilicity of PEI-M was slightly improved after the modification of PEI on Control-M and showed the water contact angle of 62.2°. Significantly, the N-C-dots/PEI-M displayed an apparent improvement in wettability with the lowest water contact angle of 43.6°. The decrease in contact angle suggested that a considerable amount of hydrophilic amine and related carboxyl groups anchored to the membrane surface. The AFM results shown in Fig. 3D indicate that the Control-M had relatively high surface roughness (123 nm, Ra). The PEI-M showed a slight improvement in surface roughness (102 nm, Ra), suggesting that the PEI was not totally conformally attached to the membrane surface but filled up part of surface depression. Most importantly, N-C-dots/PEI-M exhibited a smoothest surface topography with the surface roughness of 84 nm (Ra), which should be because that the N-C-dots/PEI made up for the valleys of the membrane surface. The lowest surface roughness of N-C-dots/PEI-M implied less bacterial adhesion among the three pieces of membranes, which is expected to improve the antibacterial properties of the membrane.

The FO performances of the Control-M, PEI-M, and N-C-dots/PEI-M were described in Fig. 4. The result shows that modified TFC membranes exhibited much higher water fluxes, especially for the N-C-dots/PEI-M, compared with Control-M (Fig. 4A). This behavior was in line with the contact angle tests, and the improved hydrophilicity mainly contributed to the water flux result. In addition, the Js values, 6.5 g m−2 h−1 for Control-M, 9.55 g m−2 h−1 MH for PEI-M, and 8.25 g m−2 h−1 for N-C-dots/PEI-M were calculated out (Fig. 4B), following a trade-off relationship between the water permeability and salt rejection. These results are possibly derived from the dilation of the membrane in a high salinity environment and for long-lived operation in FO mode.

Table 1 lists the intrinsic separation properties of the Control-M, PEI-M, and N-C-dots/PEI-M including water permeability (A), salt permeability (B), salt rejection (RS), etc. (Calculation of intrinsic transport parameters in SI). It was found that both PEI-M (1.69 L m−2 h−1 Bar−1) and N-C-dots/PEI-M (1.89 L m−2 h−1 Bar−1) displayed better permeability than Control-M (1.49 L m−2 h−1 Bar−1). The analysis of the salt permeability (B), and corresponding ratio (B/A) of salt and water permeability of Control-M, PEI-M, and N-C-dots/PEI-M gave a similar trend to the FO results. Besides, structural parameter (S) values of the Control-M, PEI-M, and N-C-dots/PEI-M Control-M are 461, 316, and 281 µm, respectively. It was obvious that the conjugation of hydrophilic PEI or N-C-dots/PEI onto TFC FO membrane beneficially reduced S, and correspondingly weakened ICP. In RO mode, the salt rejections, 94.58% for Control-M, 91.87% for PEI-M, and 93.46% for N-C-dots/PEI, were determined.

In comparison with the reported commercialized TFC FO membranes reported, our N-C-dots/PEI-M showed a relatively outstanding FO performance. Especially for the comparison with our previous report [56], the properties parameters of N-C-dots/PEI-M such as water flux, salt rejection, and S parameter, etc., are almost superior to those of IPA-C-dots/PLL-M (isophthalic acid, IPA; polylysine, PLL), demonstrating the excellent FO properties of N-C-dots/PEI-M (Table S3 in SI).

As mentioned above, N-C-dots possessed oxidase-like activity and oxygen photosensitization double enzyme mimics activities under light irradiation, with an O2 -ˑ and 1O2 -mediated catalytic process. These two ROS can highly efficiently cause cell apoptosis. The most important element in cell damage is thought to be the creation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can create oxidative stress in the cell system and cause the destruction of cell components like cell membrane, DNA and enzymes thereby causing cell apoptosis [58]. E. coli was chosen as a model bacteria to evaluate and compare the antibacterial activities of the Control-M, PEI-M and N-C-dots/PEI-M. The details on biofouling resistance are provided (4. Determination of antibacterial properties in SI). Fig. 5 shows the bacteria grown on the agar plates of three pieces of membranes. Two types of trends can be found: One is for the selfsame membrane, E. coli on the agar plate in darkness were more than those under light irradiation. The other is for the different membranes, in darkness, E. coli on the agar plates of PEI-M and N-C-dots/PEI-M were far less than those of Control-M, which is considered to be attributed to the effect of the cations from PEI and N-C-dots/PEI. Under light irradiation, the number of E. coli on the agar plates of PEI-M and N-C-dots/PEI-M were far less than those of Control-M, exhibiting the stacking effect of cations and N-C-dots nanozyme, especially for the N-C-dots/PEI-M. The results above demonstrate the excellent dual-mode antibacterial properties of N-C-dots mainly contributed to the dramatic decrease of bacteria on the N-C-dots/PEI-M (Scheme 1D).

The dynamic biofouling behavior of the Control-M, PEI-M, and N-C-dots/PEI-M was further assessed under light irradiation by a long-term FO fouling test with E. coli as model bacteria. As shown in Fig. 6, with E. coli suspension as the feed solution, each membrane maintained a slight increase in water flux within 180 min, which can be because that microorganisms were not attached onto the membrane surface. Due to the lack of antibacterial ability, the water flux of the Control-M began to slowly and then sharply decrease. Additionally, the water fluxes of the membrane kept different stable times, 550 min for PEI-M and 700 min for N-C-dots/PEI-M were observed. Because of the excellent anti-biofouling properties, the N-C-dots/PEI-M maintained stable for longer time, which was in good agreement with the static antibacterial results. Subsequently, the water fluxes of the PEI-M and N-C-dots/PEI-M sharply decreased due to the generation of biofilm. These results respond to membranes' static antibacteria and intrinsic transport properties (Table 1, Figs. 5 and 6). After cell apoptosis, the introduction of N-C-dots on the PA layer improves the surface hydrophilicity, the nitrogen atoms on N-C-dots and PEI can attract abundant water molecules to form a hydrated layer onto the membrane surface, which hinders the adsorption of apoptotic cells [59]. Compared with Control-M and PEI-M, N-C-dots/PEI-M's water flux significantly delayed the decrease time by 550 min and 200 min, respectively. This indicates that the antibacterial performance of the TFC membrane modified with N-C-dots and PEI had been significantly improved. However, the process of coupling N-C-dots to the membrane need to consider the balance between the properties and other aspects, for example, the number of N-C-dots that can be coupled on the membrane is limited. Nevertheless, compared with coupling PEI to the membrane, the water flux of N-C-dots/PEI-M remained longer time, which is still a significant improvement.

Fig. 6 B shows the water flux of the control and modified membranes before dynamic antibacteria and after the cleaning processes. dynamic antibacterial test, the N-C-dots/PEI-M displayed the maximal Jw than the other two membranes. The Jw decline of Control-M is about 21.03% after physical cleaning. However, the N-C-dots/PEI-M not only displays very high water flux, but also the decline of water flux is only 14.5%. The result shows that the carbon dot had excellent antibacterial properties and improved membrane properties.

The clean membrane samples were stabilized for 1 h using DI water as the feed and drawing solution in FO mode before the dynamic sewage treatment simulation test was conducted in FO mode. Prior to the fouling stage, the DI water and NaCl aqueous solution were utilized as the feed and absorb solution, respectively, to measure the initial water flux. The scaling phase of the synthetic wastewater containing 400 ppm of sodium alginate (SA) as a feed solution was exercised for 800 min. In this stage, the cross-flow rate of all feeds and stretching solutions was 500 mL/min. In order to estimate the reversibility of fouling, the physical cleaning phase was performed immediately after the fouling using 0.15 mM of NaCl solution as a feed one, and it circulated for 30 min with a cross-flow speed of 500 mL/min. After that, the water flux of the polluted film was measured again. At the above stage, the concentration of the NaCl draw solution was 2.0 M.

The results of the dynamic sewage treatment test of Control-M, PEI-M and N-C/PE-M were displayed in Fig. 7A. It should be noted that the reductions in water flux of Control-M and PEI-M were approximately ∼65% and ∼63%, respectively, during the 800 min test, while that of N-C-dots/PEI-M is significantly lower (∼46%). Fig. 7 B shows the water flux of the control and modified membranes before the fouling and after the cleaning processes, respectively. Before fouling, due to the excellent hydrophilicity, the N-C-dots/PEI-M displayed the maximal Jw than the other two membranes. The Jw decline of Control-M is about ∼26.5% after physical cleaning. Comparatively, the N-C-dots/PEI-M not only displays very high water flux, but also the decline of water flux is only 14.5%. This phenomenon coincides with the dynamic antibacterial conclusion. Moreover, the existence of N-C-dots hinders the accumulation of pollutants on the membrane surface in terms of stereo-hindrance. On the other hand, N-C-dots make the membrane surface smooth and reduce its fouling propensity [60]. In a word, our N-C-dots/PEI-M hold the advantages as follows: 1) N-C-dots exhibited oxidase-like and oxygen photosensitization activities; 2) N-C-dots/PEI-M was capable of keeping off the formation of biofilm by dual-mode antibacterial approach; 3) N-C-dots/PEI-M had much higher efficiency for treating with sewage, not only exhibiting an excellent model for membrane anti-bacteria, but also opening up a promising door for efficiently lengthening membrane lifetime and correspondingly reducing operation costs.

In summary, we successfully synthesized N-C-dots using Na2EDTA and urea as co-precursors through a thermal polymerization approach. The N-C-dots/PEI-M was prepared by integration of N-C-dots onto the surface of the TFC FO membrane and tested in the FO process. The photo physicochemical properties of the synthesized N-C-dots were characterized and discussed. Based on the investigation of the mechanism of anti-fouling, the N-C-dots exhibited simultaneous oxidase-like activity and oxygen sensitization functionalization under light irradiation, implying that N-C-dots owned an enormous potential for anti-biofouling. Therefore, the N-C-dots/PEI-M exhibited unique dual-mode antibacterial properties as oxidase mimics and photosensitizer for highly efficient biofouling resistance. Results of the FO test reveal that the water permeability and S parameter of N-C-dots/PEI-M was higher than, and much smaller than those of the Control-M and PEI-M, respectively. Generally, N-doped carbon dots conjugated onto the active layer can resolve the surface modification contradiction between anti-biofouling and separation performance of the TFC FO membrane.

Wen-Sheng Zou: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Pingping Wu: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Bihua Xiao: Data curation. Xia Chen: Data curation. Weili Kong: Data curation, Funding acquisition. Yaru Li: Funding acquisition. Zhaoming Liu: Supervision. Yaqin Wang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Anhui Education Department (2022AH050239) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project (2017M612091). The authors also thank PhD research startup foundation of Anhui Jianzhu University (2020QDZ23) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (52003003) for ﬁnancial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2023.109468.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para:

.
